blurb: Researchers disclosed "SearchLeak," a three-stage prompt-injection attack against Microsoft Copilot that enabled one-click theft of user data through hidden URLs and other manipulated variables. Microsoft has patched the critical flaw, but it belongs to a growing class of AI prompt-injection issues that exploit indirect inputs to exfiltrate sensitive information.

title: DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act
blurb: The Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com websites, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women, in what appears to be the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The law, enacted in 2025, criminalizes the distribution of nonconsensual intimate imagery and requires platforms to remove flagged content within 48 hours.

title: US clampdown on Anthropic models sends EU sovereignty surge into overdrive
blurb: The EU is intensifying its push for technological sovereignty after US export controls restricted European access to Anthropic's models. Brussels officials cited the curbs as evidence that the bloc must reduce dependence on American AI providers, accelerating investment in domestic compute, models, and infrastructure to insulate critical systems from foreign policy shifts.

title: Critical Copilot vulnerability allowed hackers to seal 2FA code from users
blurb: A critical vulnerability in Microsoft Copilot, dubbed SearchLeak, let attackers steal users' two-factor authentication codes by manipulating the assistant into exfiltrating sensitive data. The exploit illustrates a recurring pattern in which LLM-integrated products inherit prompt injection and data-leakage risks that the industry's current security approach repeatedly fails to prevent.

title: Feds freaked over Fable 5 after simple 'fix this code' prompt, not jailbreak, says researcher
blurb: A security researcher said federal officials raised alarms over the Fable 5 model after a simple "fix this code" prompt, not an actual jailbreak. The claim, attributed to the one person who reportedly read the underlying research paper, suggests the government response was triggered by routine model behavior rather than any deliberate exploit.

title: White House forces Anthropic to disable new frontier models following abrupt export ban
blurb: The U.S. Commerce Department issued a sweeping export control order that forced Anthropic to disable foreign access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5, two frontier models it had launched days earlier. The order arrived just before the weekend, cutting off international users with little notice and signaling tighter federal control over who can use the most capable American AI systems abroad.

title: France plans €655M in AI investments through 2030 and a Mistral-powered chatbot for state services; its security service will replace Palantir with Chapsvision (Benoit Berthelot/Bloomberg)
blurb: France plans to invest €655M in AI through 2030 and will deploy a Mistral-powered chatbot across state services. Its domestic intelligence agency, DGSI, will replace Palantir's data tools with software from local vendor Chapsvision, part of a broader push toward sovereign AI infrastructure.

title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
blurb: Anthropic abruptly suspended all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after receiving a US export control directive barring foreign nationals from using the models. The order followed abuse concerns flagged by regulators, cutting off international users with no advance notice and raising questions about how export rules will apply to frontier AI access going forward.

title: Trump Administration Reignites Its Feud With Anthropic Over Latest A.I. Models
blurb: The Trump administration imposed surprise restrictions Friday that cut off foreign access to Anthropic's latest AI models, reigniting its conflict with the company. The move triggered another round of finger pointing between the administration and Anthropic over the rules governing model exports.

title: Anthropic’s new privacy policy offers US consumers a way around the Fable ban
blurb: The US government ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its new Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all foreign nationals on national security grounds, but the company says it cannot distinguish foreign users from US citizens, forcing a blanket shutdown. An update to its consumer privacy policy, taking effect July 8, gives Anthropic a way to identify US customers and restore their access.

title: Cybersecurity experts don’t think Anthropic’s Fable 5 presents a unique threat
blurb: Cybersecurity practitioners said Anthropic's Fable 5 does not provide unique hacking capabilities, with dozens calling proposed export controls on the model's foreign use misguided. Recent jailbreak reports failed to demonstrate offensive capabilities beyond what existing models and tools already offer, undercutting the case that Fable 5 warrants distinct regulatory treatment.

title: Can Europe train a frontier AI model on the compute it owns?
blurb: EuroMesh is an open-source project that maps the GPU capacity Europe already controls and models whether that pooled compute could train a frontier-scale model without relying on US hyperscalers. The analysis argues that fragmentation across national labs and clouds, not raw hardware, is the binding constraint, and that coordination could close most of the gap with leading American training runs.

title: Microsoft sued over performance of Azure business
blurb: A Michigan pension fund filed a class action lawsuit against Microsoft, alleging the company misled investors about the costs of its AI expansion and the performance of its Azure cloud business. The suit claims Microsoft withheld the full picture of capital spending tied to AI infrastructure, leaving shareholders without an accurate view of the margins and demand underpinning Azure.

title: Study: Mistral and other open-source AI models are among the weakest at filtering Russian disinformation; Mistral's top model ranks 47 out of 60 tested models (Andrew Jack/Financial Times)
blurb: A study by Estonian researchers found that open-source generative models are the weakest at filtering Russian disinformation, with Mistral's top model ranking 47th out of 60 tested. The findings suggest open-weight systems lag proprietary models at removing false news, raising scrutiny over how these models handle state-sponsored propaganda.

title: Process-Oriented Evaluation of AI-Assisted Scientific Writing
blurb: This paper analyzes 869 keystroke-level edit logs and finds AI-generated abstracts gain sentence-level agency but lag human-authored ones on global coherence.

title: Claude Corps
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Corps, a program pairing its engineers with organizations to build and deploy Claude-powered tools.

title: Anthropic, Trump Officials Seek Deal on Restoring Powerful Model Access
blurb: Anthropic and Trump administration officials are negotiating to restore access to the company's Fable and Mythos models after Anthropic pulled them. In its first public comments since the removal, Anthropic said both parties are working quickly to resolve the matter, though neither side detailed what prompted the loss of access or the terms under discussion.

title: Exclusive: Mindbeam touts dramatic performance improvements in CPU-based AI inference
blurb: Mindbeam released Litespark-Inference, an open-source framework that runs ternary LLMs efficiently on standard consumer CPUs, reducing GPU reliance for some workloads.

title: The Anatomy of Scam Scenarios: Large-Scale Characterization and Conversation-Aware Detection
blurb: A study of 102,054 real-world scams maps 18 scenarios into 6 tactics and builds conversation-aware detection for financial customer interactions.

title: Meta is testing smart glass facial recognition tech that’s also used by police and military: Report
blurb: Meta licensed face recognition from Pentagon contractor Rank One and built NameTag into an app on 50 million phones before deleting it.

title: As AI agents become employees, NewCore emerges with $66M to give them identities
blurb: NewCore launched with $66M to manage identities for AI agents, arguing agent identity will define enterprise security.

title: DocLang aims to make documents readable by AI, not humans
blurb: IBM, Nvidia, and Red Hat launched DocLang, a Linux Foundation-hosted open spec for AI-native business documents built for LLM tokenizers.

title: How Much Do Reviews Really Contribute? A Study on Text-Enriched Matrix Factorization for Recommendations
blurb: A study testing whether textual reviews actually improve matrix factorization recommendations, comparing gating and cross-attention strategies for fusing collaborative and review signals.

title: Rio de Janeiro's city government model Rio3.5 beats Qwen3.7 in recent benchmarks
blurb: Rio de Janeiro's city government released Rio3.5, a model that outperforms Qwen3.7 on recent benchmarks.

title: Anthropic’s AI Runs Into DC Rule Inconsistency
blurb: Anthropic is working to address White House national security concerns after its Mythos and Fable models triggered a policy reversal in Washington. The episode highlights inconsistent federal rules governing frontier AI, leaving the company to navigate shifting expectations while it negotiates terms that would keep the models in compliance.

title: Salesforce acquires Fin, formerly known as Intercom
blurb: Salesforce acquired Fin, the customer service platform formerly known as Intercom, adding to its run of agentic AI deals.

title: Introducing Genie ZeroOps: Put your data and AI operations on autopilot
blurb: Databricks introduced Genie ZeroOps, which automates data and AI operations like pipeline maintenance to reduce manual upkeep.

title: GPU infrastructure management startup Hydra Host raises $100M
blurb: Hydra Host raised $100M in Series A funding led by Kindred Ventures, with Nvidia, Ark Invest, and Founders Fund participating.

title: SpaceX’s biggest-ever IPO just grew to $85.7 billion raised
blurb: SpaceX's IPO grew to $85.7 billion raised after underwriters maxed out their share purchases, making it the largest public offering on record. The buy-up adds to an already historic total, pushing demand for the launch and Starlink business well past initial expectations. A raise of this size resets the ceiling for what a private company can pull from public markets, and signals that investor appetite for hard-tech and space infrastructure now rivals the demand once reserved for software.

title: SpaceX is public: Everything you need to know post-IPO
blurb: SpaceX completed its IPO, and the S-1 finally puts numbers behind a company that has spent two decades as a private black box. The filing reveals the real split between the launch business and Starlink, who cashed in on pre-IPO secondary deals, and which early backers stand to win or lose now that the shares trade publicly. For founders, the bigger signal is that the most capital-intensive hard-tech bet of the era reached the public markets, which reopens a path that software-era venture had all but written off.

title: SpaceX to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after blockbuster IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60B in all-stock, days after Cursor's blockbuster IPO, to shore up its struggling AI division. The pitch to IPO investors leaned on a claimed $26 trillion addressable market in AI, a number that has to absorb a frontier coding tool bolted onto a rocket company. The deal signals that the largest private hardware players now see AI tooling as core infrastructure worth paying public-market premiums to own outright, even when the strategic fit is far from obvious.

title: SpaceX market cap tops $2 trillion after shares of Elon Musk's rocket company gain 19% on debut
blurb: SpaceX debuted on the Nasdaq Friday with shares jumping 19%, pushing its market cap past $2 trillion and making it one of the most valuable public companies in the world. The listing hands Elon Musk a massive new pool of liquid equity to fund Starship and Starlink expansion, and it sets a benchmark valuation no other private space or defense startup can touch. For founders, it confirms that capital-intensive hard-tech can command software-scale multiples when a company owns its market outright.

title: SpaceX IPO raises total of $85.7 billion as underwriters exercise 'greenshoe' overallotment option
blurb: SpaceX's IPO now totals $85.7 billion after underwriters exercised their greenshoe overallotment option, up from the $75 billion raised Thursday. That makes it the largest public offering on record, and it values Musk's combined space and AI business above almost every company that has ever gone public. The size of the raise signals that capital is again flowing to hard-tech and frontier infrastructure at a scale software founders rarely see, with rockets and compute, not apps, drawing the biggest checks in the market right now.

title: Docs: audited financial figures show OpenAI spent $34B in 2025, up 172% YoY, including $19B on research and development and nearly $6B on sales and marketing (Ed Zitron/Ed Zitron's Where's Your Ed At)
blurb: OpenAI spent $34B in 2025 according to audited figures, up 172% year over year, with $19B going to research and development and nearly $6B to sales and marketing. The R&D line is the one to watch, because it signals a company still buying its way to the frontier rather than harvesting a settled lead. For founders building on top of OpenAI, that spend pace is both the reason the models keep improving and the reason the supplier above you needs to keep monetizing the stack you sit on.

title: SpaceX Agrees to Buy AI Coding Agent Cursor for $60 Billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to buy AI coding agent Cursor for $60B, its first major acquisition since last week's IPO. The deal signals that the frontier of acquirers is widening beyond the labs and hyperscalers, with cash-rich operators now paying software-rivaling prices for autonomous coding tools to fold into their own engineering stacks. For founders building on top of coding agents, the takeaway is that the category is consolidating into strategic hands fast, and the window to stay independent narrows every time a buyer this size writes a check.

title: SpaceX Cements $60 Billion Cursor Takeover Following IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to take over Cursor at a $60 billion valuation, formalizing Elon Musk's push to close the gap with rivals on AI coding tools. The deal lands right after Cursor's IPO and folds one of the fastest-growing coding startups into Musk's orbit. It signals that the frontier AI coding race is now consolidating into the hands of the largest players, leaving independent tools to choose a camp or get bought.

title: SpaceX Surges for Second Day to Add $412 Billion in Value
blurb: SpaceX climbed for a second straight day after its record IPO, adding $412 billion in market value and instantly ranking among the world's most valuable public companies. The debut hands SpaceX a deep, liquid currency for acquisitions, talent, and the capital-hungry buildout of Starship and Starlink. For founders, it also resets the exit math: a private company can now stay private for two decades and still print one of the largest public listings ever, proof that the patient, capital-intensive path can win.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to buy Anysphere, the maker of the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B, pulling a frontier dev-tools company under a hardware and aerospace umbrella. The deal signals that the buyers with the deepest pockets for AI talent and tooling are no longer the cloud and software incumbents but cash-rich operators from adjacent industries. For founders building on coding agents, it also concentrates one of the category leaders inside a company with its own engineering agenda, raising the odds that Cursor's roadmap starts serving its parent before the broader market.

title: SpaceX stock jumps 20% in first full day of trading after record debut
blurb: SpaceX rose 20% in its first full day of public trading, pushing its valuation past $2 trillion after a record IPO debut. The jump makes SpaceX one of the most valuable companies in the world and gives Elon Musk a publicly traded counterweight to Tesla. For founders, it resets the ceiling on what a capital-intensive hardware company can be worth, after a decade of investors favoring asset-light software over launch vehicles and satellites.

title: As AI agents become employees, NewCore emerges with $66M to give them identities
blurb: NewCore launched with $66M to issue and govern identities for AI agents, betting that the next enterprise security problem is managing software workers rather than human ones. As agents start taking actions inside systems the way employees do, they need credentials, permissions, and audit trails that the existing identity stack was never built to handle. For founders, it marks the start of a new infrastructure layer forming around agentic AI, where access control and accountability become the choke point that determines how far companies are willing to deploy agents.

title: SEC filing: SpaceX agrees to acquire Cursor in a merger valuing the AI coding startup at $60B, set to close in Q3 2026, or pay a $4B to $10B termination fee (Reuters)
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the company behind AI coding agent Cursor, in a merger valuing the startup at $60B, with the deal set to close in Q3 2026. An SEC filing puts the breakup fee at $4B to $10B if it falls through, a band that signals how hard both sides expect regulators to push. The bigger signal is that frontier-adjacent industrial players are now buying their way into the AI dev-tools stack, not just partnering, and the price of a category-leading coding agent has reset to rocket-company scale.

title: DeepSeek Becomes China’s Most Valuable AI Startup After $7.4 Billion Fundraise
blurb: DeepSeek raised more than $7.4 billion in its first outside round, making it China's most valuable AI startup. The round funds the kind of compute-heavy training that has gotten far more expensive since the company's cheap-to-run models first rattled the market last year. China now has a clearly capitalized national champion to put against the US frontier labs, and the cost narrative that made DeepSeek famous matters less than the war chest it just built.

title: Space Stocks Dip After SpaceX IPO
blurb: SpaceX's debut went well, but the rest of the public space sector sold off the same day, with major listed players dropping across the board. The move suggests investors are treating SpaceX as the place to own space exposure and rotating out of everyone else rather than bidding the whole category up. For founders in adjacent hardware-heavy markets, it is a reminder that a dominant player going public can pull capital toward itself and away from the rest of the field.

title: Salesforce acquires AI customer service platform Fin for $3.6B
blurb: Salesforce acquired Fin, an AI customer service platform, for $3.6B, folding its team and technology into Agentforce. The deal buys Salesforce a proven autonomous support agent rather than building one from scratch, and it deepens the bet that customer service is the first enterprise workflow AI agents fully own. Expect the frontier of competition in CRM to keep shifting from software seats to agents that resolve tickets, with pricing tied to outcomes instead of headcount.

title: Fox to acquire Roku in $22B deal
blurb: Fox agreed to acquire Roku for $22B, a move it says creates the third-largest TV company in the US. The deal hands Fox a streaming distribution layer and a connected-TV ad business it lacked, while Roku's platform suddenly sits inside a traditional broadcaster rather than competing as a neutral aggregator. For founders building on streaming or CTV ad infrastructure, the consolidation narrows the field of independent platforms and tilts leverage further toward the few companies that now own both content and the screen it plays on.

title: AI agent authorization startup Arcade nabs $60M investment
blurb: Arcade AI raised $60M in a Series A led by SYN Ventures, with Morgan Stanley and Wipro joining, to build authorization infrastructure for AI agents. As agents move from demos into production, the unsolved problem is letting them act on a user's behalf across third-party services without handing over standing credentials or broad access. The round signals that identity and permissioning are becoming a distinct layer of the agent stack, and the founders building agentic products will need to answer how their software authenticates before customers let it touch real systems.

title: Sarvam becomes India’s newest AI unicorn with $234 million funding round led by HCLTech
blurb: Sarvam joined India's unicorn club with a $234M round led by HCLTech, which is putting in $150M of the total. The deal ties the Bengaluru startup to one of India's largest IT services firms, giving it an enterprise distribution channel and a strategic backer rather than a purely financial one. India's AI buildout is increasingly running through its IT incumbents, which are funding homegrown model labs to defend their services business against the same automation they sell.

title: ChatGPT’s market share slips below 50% for first time
blurb: ChatGPT's share of the AI assistant market fell below 50% for the first time, even as it stays the most-used product with over 1.1 billion monthly users. Gemini now sits at 662 million and Claude at 245 million, so the gap is closing from both the consumer and enterprise sides. The takeaway for founders building on top of these models is that the assistant layer is becoming a real multi-horse race, which changes distribution math, default integrations, and who you bet your roadmap on.

title: Malaysia’s AI agent-powered messaging app Respond.io raises $62.5M, eyes acquisitions
blurb: Respond.io, one of Malaysia's startups to watch, raised $62.5M to fund acquisitions across North America and Europe. The messaging platform runs AI agents that handle high volumes of customer inquiries and charges per conversation rather than per seat, a pricing model that ties cost to resolved work instead of headcount. The round signals that the per-conversation model is starting to pull capital toward emerging-market AI infrastructure, and that consolidation in the customer-messaging space is now being driven from outside Silicon Valley.

title: The hidden pattern behind successful products | Mark Pincus (founder of Zynga)
blurb: Successful products almost never come from a genuinely new idea. Mark Pincus argues your instincts about what to build are usually right, but the specific idea is usually wrong, so the move is to start from something already proven and make it meaningfully better before reaching for something new. His "Proven, Better, New" framework puts the odds in that order: take a validated category, find the dimension where you can win, and treat pure novelty as the riskiest bet rather than the goal.

title: ChapsVision to replace Palantir in major contract with French intelligence agency
blurb: France's domestic intelligence agency is dropping Palantir and handing the contract to ChapsVision, a homegrown French data-analysis firm. The move is the latest sign that European governments increasingly treat dependence on US software as a strategic liability, not just a procurement choice. For founders building in defense, govtech, or any regulated sector, sovereignty is becoming a feature buyers will pay for, and incumbents with the deepest tech can still lose on where their code is written and who controls it.

title: How to earn a billion dollars
blurb: The reliable way to earn a billion dollars is to start a company that grows, because wealth comes from owning a large share of something that scales, not from being paid for your time. Salary caps what you can make, while equity in a fast-growing startup compounds without a ceiling. The essay's point for founders is that the size of the outcome tracks the value you create multiplied by how much of it you keep.

title: Not everyone is using AI for everything
blurb: Most people who try AI use it occasionally, and a large share of the population isn't using it at all. Adoption has barely moved over the past year, despite the assumption that AI is now woven into everyone's daily work. The notable shift is in sentiment, with negative feelings about AI climbing as many people cite real concerns and see little perceived value in it.

title: State Attorneys General Are Investigating OpenAI
blurb: A coalition of state attorneys general has opened an investigation into OpenAI, probing its handling of user data, the safety of minors, and its move into advertising. The scope matters because it spans the parts of the business OpenAI is leaning on for growth, from the consumer data that trains and personalizes its products to the ad model it is just starting to build. Regulatory pressure at the state level tends to move faster and more unpredictably than federal action, and it lands on every company building on top of OpenAI's platform too.

title: Anthropic’s Safety Superpower
blurb: Anthropic's deep conviction in its own safety mission has become a business weapon, justifying moves that favor its commercial interests and even put it at odds with the U.S. government. The author argues that certainty about being the good actor gives a company license to act aggressively, because every decision can be framed as serving safety. The risk is that a safety identity becomes a blank check that few inside the company will question.

title: Claude earns more revenue per user than ChatGPT, Sensor Tower finds
blurb: OpenAI still leads AI on raw size, with ChatGPT's apps crossing 1 billion monthly users in May, the fastest any app has reached that mark, per Sensor Tower's State of AI 2026 report. But on the metric that pays the bills, Anthropic's Claude is now earning more revenue per user than ChatGPT. The split matters because scale and monetization are diverging: OpenAI is winning the land grab while Claude is quietly building the better unit economics, and over time it is margin, not user count, that decides who can keep funding the next model.

title: European tech has discovered some swagger
blurb: European tech has spent years apologizing for not being Silicon Valley, building quietly and pricing modestly while founders defaulted to the assumption that the real action happened elsewhere. That posture is fading, as a generation of operators who have built and sold at scale now start companies expecting to win globally rather than get acquired early. The shift that matters is not capital or talent, which Europe already had, but the willingness to make ambitious bets out loud and defend them.

title: SpaceX Set to Overtake Amazon in Value as It Soars for Third Day
blurb: SpaceX is on track to pass Amazon as the world's fifth-largest public company, with shares up more than 50% across three sessions since its debut. The run puts a launch-and-satellite business above one of the defining platforms of the internet era, on the strength of Starlink's recurring revenue and Starship's payload economics. The market is now pricing space infrastructure as a core franchise rather than a moonshot, which resets what a credible hard-tech valuation looks like for every founder raising in the category.

title: What new AI search data reveals about visibility and trust
blurb: Buyers no longer trust a single source, they validate the same claim across search, social, marketplaces, and AI assistants before they commit. New data shows AI systems pull authority from the places that show up consistently across those surfaces, so a strong ranking on one channel no longer carries the decision. The practical move is to seed consistent, citable information everywhere your buyers check, because visibility in AI answers now depends on agreement across platforms, not dominance of any one.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: Ahrefs analyzed 137K domains and found 97% of llms.txt files never get read by any bot.

title: May Core Update Moves Reddit To Google’s Default Answer for Important Questions
blurb: Google's May 2026 core update now routes experience-based queries to Reddit, per SE Ranking's analysis of 100,000 keywords across 20 niches.

title: Fox Buys Roku, The Problem With Fox’s Smart Strategy, Streaming That Works
blurb: Wall Street hated Fox's Roku deal, but the logic is a bet on a different position in the stack. Fox is trading the leverage it holds as a rights extractor for the leverage of owning distribution, becoming a renter of content with a direct path to viewers. The market reads that as giving up a strong hand, while the strategy reads as buying the layer where streaming margins actually accrue.

title: “단일 AI 모델=새로운 단일 장애 지점”…앤트로픽 접근 차단이 던진 경고
blurb: Anthropic disabled its top models, Fable 5 and Mithos 5, for every customer last Friday after the US government issued an export-control order barring foreign nationals from using them. The directive arrived with no specific justification and gave the company no time to appeal, which means a vendor relationship a CIO vetted for ethics and reliability can be severed by a third party overnight. If your stack depends on one model, you have built a single point of failure, and the smart play is keeping a second provider you can route to.

title: Configuration Smells in AGENTS.md Files: Common Mistakes in Configuring Coding Agents
blurb: This paper presents the first catalog of "configuration smells" in AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, the instruction files that steer coding agents on architecture, conventions, and testing. After mining repositories and reviewing 100 popular open-source projects, the authors define six smells with automated detection heuristics, finding Lint Leakage (linter rules duplicated into agent instructions) the most common at 62% of files.

title: Banned Book Library in a Wi-Fi Smart Light Bulb
blurb: A developer flashed a Wi-Fi smart bulb's ESP8266 microcontroller to host a hidden library of banned books served over a local web page. The post walks through dumping the stock firmware, repurposing the chip's flash storage to hold the text, and serving it via a captive portal that anyone nearby can connect to without internet access.

title: The time the x86 emulator team found code so bad they fixed it during emulation
blurb: Raymond Chen recounts how Windows' x86-on-ARM emulation team hit application code so pathologically inefficient that the just-in-time translator effectively rewrote it on the fly, producing emulated output that ran better than the original native instructions. The post uses the case to explain how binary translation can recognize and collapse redundant instruction patterns, turning a quirk of bad code into a window onto how the emulator's optimizer actually works.

title: LLM-as-Code Agentic Programming for Agent Harness
blurb: This paper argues that token explosion, control-flow hallucination, and unreliable completion in LLM agents are architectural consequences of making a probabilistic model orchestrate deterministic looping, branching, and sequencing. The authors propose Agentic Programming, where the program owns all control flow and the LLM is invoked as an adaptive component only for reasoning or generation, with each call's context built from the execution history's call tree as a DAG.

title: Game Engine White Papers: Commander Keen
blurb: This post dissects how Commander Keen pulled off smooth side-scrolling on 1990 PC hardware that was never designed for it, using John Carmack's adaptive tile refresh to redraw only the screen cells that changed between frames. The write-up reconstructs the EGA memory tricks, off-screen page buffering, and tile bookkeeping that let the engine hit console-grade scrolling where competitors settled for full-screen redraws.

title: Organizational Cohesion in Microservice Architectures: A Multi-Project Empirical Study
blurb: This paper introduces organizational cohesion, a measure of how closely developer contributions align with microservice boundaries, operationalized as Pairwise Team Cohesion (PTC) by adapting the Sensitive Class Cohesion Metric. The authors validate it through a longitudinal study of Netflix's Spinnaker platform and six other open-source microservice systems, finding systematic divergences between service decomposition and the team structures that actually maintain those services.

title: I indexed 669 GB of my GoPro videos using my M1 Max computer and local ML models
blurb: A developer indexed 669 GB of personal GoPro footage entirely on an M1 Max using local ML models, building a searchable archive without sending video to any cloud service. The pipeline extracts frames, runs them through on-device vision models to generate embeddings and captions, and stores the results so clips can be retrieved by natural-language queries.

title: SPARK: Security Knowledge Priming and Representation-Guided Knowledge Activation for LLM-based Secure Code Generation
blurb: SPARK is an inference-time security harness that improves LLM secure code generation by retrieving relevant CWE entries and appending a short structured cue to the prompt, activating latent security knowledge without any retraining. The authors argue pretraining corpora already contain rich security material and that the real bottleneck is activation, positioning SPARK against heavier defenses like fine-tuning or external knowledge retrieval that add compute overhead and data bias.

title: Agent trajectories as programs: fingerprinting and programming coding-agent behavior
blurb: This paper introduces methods for fingerprinting coding agents by their procedural behavior rather than benchmark scores, using a compressive vocabulary-induction technique to represent how an agent solves problems. Across ten agents on SWE-Bench, a probe over these procedural signatures attributes an unseen trajectory to the correct agent with 85.7% accuracy, and behavior clusters most tightly among models from similar release periods.

title: Snyk VulnBench JS 1.0: Can LLMs Find the Same Bugs Twice?
blurb: This benchmark ran 300 repeated agentic LLM security scans on identical JavaScript, prompts, and harness, finding that reference-matched vulnerabilities were highly repeatable (134 of 158 appeared in all five runs) while unmatched extra findings were not, with 80 of 161 surfacing in only one of five repetitions. The authors argue LLM review and Snyk's deterministic SAST are complementary: models reliably catch high-signal exploit shapes, while static analysis is better at systematically enumerating repeated data-flow sinks.

title: How TimescaleDB compresses time-series data
blurb: The post breaks down TimescaleDB's Hypercore engine, which converts row-based chunks into a columnar format by packing up to 1,000 rows into a single compressed batch and applying per-type schemes like delta-of-delta, Gorilla, and dictionary encoding. It covers how segment-by and order-by configuration shapes the compression ratio and query performance, with the trade-off that updates to compressed data are slower than on uncompressed chunks.

title: Your ePub Is fine
blurb: **The author traces why a spec-compliant ePub gets rejected by Kobo e-readers, pinning the failure on Adobe's aging Reader Mobile SDK rather than the file itself.** The post walks through validating the ePub against the standard, reproducing Kobo's parse error, and showing how the embedded Adobe XML parser chokes on markup that EPUBCheck and other readers accept without complaint.

title: From Third-Party to First-Party: Measuring and Protecting Against Modern Web Tracking Mechanisms
blurb: A large-scale measurement of top sites found that over 54% now deploy first-party or server-side tracking, which relocates tracking logic into the browser's first-party context or the website's backend to evade traditional client-side detection. The authors build a provider-independent detection methodology and cluster scripts by similarity into a network graph, showing the tracking ecosystem persists even as it dodges conventional privacy tools.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Software Verification: Hyper-charging Local Language Models with Symbolic Reasoning at Scale
blurb: VerIbmc is a neuro-symbolic pipeline that pairs deterministic symbolic invariant synthesis with locally deployable open-weight models and the ESBMC verifier, targeting loop invariant generation without sending source code to proprietary cloud APIs. The authors evaluate five open-weight models from 7B to 120B parameters across five benchmark families, comparing Chain-of-Thought and Tree-of-Thought prompting in an iterative refinement loop driven by structured verifier feedback.

title: No Resource, No Benchmarks, No Problem? Evaluating and Improving LLMs for Code Generation in No-Resource Languages
blurb: This paper introduces three new benchmarks for evaluating LLM code generation in no-resource languages, the proprietary or domain-specific languages that companies build in-house and that models have effectively never seen in training. The authors test and improve generation for these languages, targeting the gap left by tools like GitHub Copilot that only support high-resource ecosystems such as Python and Java.

title: Building Reliable Agentic AI Systems
blurb: Thoughtcombines from keyword search to research assistant for Bayer pharma researchers.Bayer engineers built a system that lets pharmaceutical researchers query decades of study data buried in PDF reports, evolving from keyword-based search into an LLM research assistant that answers complex questions and drafts regulatory documents. The writeup details the architecture choices that made it reliable, including how the team handled retrieval over dense scientific documents and constrained the model to keep outputs grounded and auditable.

title: The Ghosts of Polymarket: When Off-Chain Matches Meet On-Chain Reverts
blurb: Researchers analyzed Polymarket's hybrid architecture, which matches orders off-chain but settles on-chain, exposing a consistency gap they call Ghost Fills, where matched orders fail at settlement. Their GHOSTHUNTER tool reconstructed 1,952,440 reverted transactions and attributed them to four attack vectors—nonce bump, balance drain, allowance revoke, and proxy trap—realized across 35 variants that invalidate orders before finalization.

title: Formal methods and the future of programming
blurb: Jane Street details how it applies formal methods across its codebase, using techniques like type-driven design, property-based testing, and verification tools to catch bugs that conventional testing misses. The series argues that lightweight formal methods are now practical for everyday production code rather than confined to academia or safety-critical systems, with OCaml's type system doing much of the heavy lifting.

title: How memory safety CVEs differ between Rust and C/C++
blurb: The author analyzes memory safety CVE data and argues that Rust's guarantees shift the nature of vulnerabilities rather than just reducing their count, since unsafe blocks and logic errors still produce memory bugs that look different from C/C++ classics. The post contends that Rust's compiler eliminates most use-after-free and buffer-overflow patterns up front, concentrating remaining issues in a smaller, auditable surface where reviewers can focus their attention.

title: 21 years and counting of 'eight fallacies of distributed computing' (2025)
blurb: The post revisits Peter Deutsch's eight fallacies of distributed computing and argues they remain accurate 21 years on, with engineers still building as if the network is reliable, latency is zero, and bandwidth is infinite. The author maps each fallacy to modern failure modes in microservices and cloud systems, noting that newer abstractions hide the network without removing its costs.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less (xpost)
blurb: Charity Majors argues that AI coding tools raise the bar for engineering discipline rather than lowering it, comparing the moment to the shift from hand-tended server pets to immutable infrastructure. She contends that faster code generation makes practices like testing, observability, and tight feedback loops more essential, since the cost of shipping unreviewed or poorly understood changes scales with how quickly you can produce them.

title: What job interviews taught me about Kubernetes
blurb: The author argues that interviewing engineers about Kubernetes exposed how often teams adopt it without grasping the fundamentals it abstracts away, treating cluster orchestration as a checkbox rather than a tool matched to a real scaling problem. The takeaway is that the people who reason best about Kubernetes can explain what it does at the level of scheduling, networking, and failure handling, not just recite kubectl commands or YAML they copied from a manifest.

title: Don't trust large context windows
blurb: The author argues that large context windows degrade model performance well before hitting their advertised token limits, with recall and reasoning falling off as the prompt fills. The practical advice is to treat context as a scarce budget, aggressively prune irrelevant tokens, and split work across focused calls rather than dumping everything into one oversized window.

title: Linux 7.1
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, the latest mainline kernel, following the standard two-week stabilization period after the final release candidate. As with past major-number bumps, the jump carries no special significance beyond keeping minor version numbers from growing unwieldy, with the bulk of the changes landing as routine driver updates, filesystem fixes, and incremental subsystem improvements.

title: Phoenix LiveView 1.2
blurb: Phoenix LiveView 1.2 introduces colocated hooks and colocated JS, which let developers write client-side JavaScript directly inside HEEx templates instead of maintaining separate hook files, with the JS extracted at compile time and bundled by the asset pipeline. The release also adds a new portal API for rendering content outside the current DOM tree, useful for modals and tooltips, and ships compatibility fixes for the latest Phoenix and Elixir versions.

title: Java's Project Valhalla finally lands a preview in JDK 28
blurb: Java's Project Valhalla lands its first preview in JDK 28, introducing value classes that drop object identity to let the JVM flatten data inline and cut memory overhead. Architect Brian Goetz cautioned that the feature will likely remain in preview through the next LTS release, so production use is still some way off.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that conforms Claude to the `LanguageModel` protocol in Apple's Foundation Models framework, so apps drive it through the same `LanguageModelSession` API used for Apple's on-device model. Requests go straight to the Claude API rather than through Apple, and developers swap between on-device and server-side models per session to add larger context, frontier reasoning, or server tools like web search and code execution.

title: Iroh 1.0
blurb: Iroh 1.0 is a Rust library for building direct peer-to-peer connections, using QUIC with NAT hole-punching and falling back to relay servers when a direct path can't be established. The 1.0 release marks a stable API and on-wire protocol, with the relay fallback meaning connections still succeed even when both peers sit behind restrictive NATs.

title: Typst 0.15.0
blurb: Typst released version 0.15.0 of its markup-based typesetting system, a faster, scriptable alternative to LaTeX that compiles documents incrementally and ships a built-in scripting language for layout, math, and figures. The release continues to target academic and technical writing with markdown-like source syntax, sub-second recompiles on edits, and a package ecosystem that replaces the sprawl of LaTeX distributions.

title: Show HN: Kage – Shadow any website to a single binary for offline viewing
blurb: Kage crawls any website and packages it into a single self-contained binary that serves the full site offline with no external dependencies. The Go tool captures HTML, CSS, JS, and assets into one executable you can run anywhere, useful for archiving docs, demoing sites without a network, or shipping a frozen snapshot.

title: Free SQL→ER diagram tool, runs in the browser, nothing uploaded
blurb: This browser-based tool converts SQL schema definitions into entity-relationship diagrams entirely client-side, with no server upload or account required. The parsing and rendering run locally, so database schemas never leave the machine, and the tool is free to use for visualizing table structures and foreign-key relationships.

title: Selection Without Signal, Recovery Through Expression: A Measurement Study of Post-Hoc Falsification Operators for Frozen Small Code Models
blurb: This paper tests whether post-hoc operators that select, verify, or repair the outputs of frozen sub-1.5B code models can beat plain Best-of-N sampling, evaluating 26 semantic operators under a leakage-free, matched-compute protocol. None improved held-out accuracy over Best-of-N, blocked by a coverage wall on hard tasks and a capability scissors that leaves too few discriminable errors among visible-test passers.

title: Caddy compatibility for zeroserve: 3x throughput and 70% lower latency
blurb: zeroserve added a compatibility layer for Caddy configs, letting users run existing Caddyfiles on its server while reporting 3x higher throughput and 70% lower latency in the author's benchmarks. The post details the drop-in migration path and where behavior diverges from Caddy, with the main trade-off being incomplete coverage of Caddy's plugin ecosystem.

title: Open-SWE-Traces: Advancing Dual-Mode Multilingual Distillation for Software Engineering Agents
blurb: Open-SWE-Traces is a dataset of 207,489 agentic coding trajectories spanning nine languages, sourced from 20,000 real-world PRs via OpenHands and SWE-agent and synthesized with both explicit-reasoning and non-thinking traces. Fine-tuning the Qwen3-30B-A3B series on the data yields resolve rates of 61.7% on SWE-bench Verified, 57.1% on SWE-bench Multilingual, and 36.8% on SWE-bench Pro.

title: Openrouter Fusion API
blurb: OpenRouter released Fusion, a single API endpoint that dynamically routes each request across multiple frontier models rather than pinning calls to one provider. The model is exposed under the standard OpenRouter chat completions interface, so existing integrations can swap to it without code changes while OpenRouter handles model selection and failover behind the slug.

title: Graphical-Probabilistic Modeling of Generative Flows in LLM-Native Software Systems
blurb: This paper proposes Generation Networks, a graphical probabilistic modeling framework for documenting the stochastic, prompt-dependent generative flows in LLM-native software systems. The authors argue that current practice relies on low-level prompting and context-engineering heuristics that lack the modularity and abstraction needed for design-level reasoning, and offer the framework as a foundation for stating and analyzing properties of LLM-based designs.

title: obliv-clang: Real-World Oblivious Programming in C++
blurb: obliv-clang is a compile-time checker that verifies the obliviousness of C++ programs, ensuring execution time and data access patterns leak no secrets to timing and access-pattern side-channel attacks. It supports rich C++ features including arbitrarily nested pointers to work with existing industry codebases, ships formally proven soundness rules, and produces high-performance binaries with minimal compilation overhead.

title: Binary Decompilation LLM with Feedback-Driven Multi-Turn Refinement
blurb: AutoDecompiler is a decompilation-specialized LLM trained with reinforcement learning that treats binary decompilation as iterative refinement rather than one-shot generation, revising its output across multiple turns based on compilation, execution, and input/output testing feedback. The approach targets a known failure mode where single-turn methods produce code that compiles and reads cleanly yet diverges from the original binary's behavior, misleading downstream security analysis like vulnerability discovery and malware inspection.

title: Accelerating researchers and developers building multilingual AI with a new open dataset
blurb: GitHub published a new repository-level dataset under CC0-1.0 that maps multilingual developer content across READMEs, issues, and pull requests. The release gives researchers and developers a public-domain corpus for training and evaluating multilingual AI models on real-world software discussions rather than English-only sources.

title: C^2: Cache-Conscious Succinct Tries with Adaptive Unary Path Compression
blurb: C² introduces two optimizations for succinct tries: a cache-friendly bitvector layout that cuts cache misses during trie navigation, and adaptive unary path compression that eliminates space wasted on redundant single-child chains. Applied to FST, CoCo-trie, and Marisa across six datasets, the layout change improves query performance by 1.12x to 1.58x while the path compression yields a 1.3x smaller memory footprint on average.

title: Arch Linux locks down AUR signups amid wave of malicious commits
blurb: Arch Linux froze new account signups on the Arch User Repository after attackers flooded it with malicious commits that poisoned package updates. The community-maintained AUR ships build scripts rather than vetted binaries, so the lockdown buys maintainers time to clean compromised packages and review the backlog before reopening registration to contributors.

title: GitHub releases an open dataset for multilingual developer content
blurb: GitHub released the GitHub Multilingual Repositories Dataset, a CC0-1.0 collection that helps researchers and developers find public repos containing non-English natural-language content across READMEs, issues, and pull request threads. The dataset fulfills a 2025 commitment GitHub made under Microsoft's European Digital Commitments and is hosted on GitHub itself.

title: North Korean Hackers Are Turning Developer Tools Into Malware Delivery Channels
blurb: Proofpoint flagged two phishing campaigns from the North Korean threat cluster Contagious Interview, which lures developers with fake recruiter outreach and code review requests before delivering malware through trusted dev tooling. The activity overlaps with the group also tracked as Famous Chollima and Void Dokkaebi, signaling that job-application and code-review workflows are now active attack surfaces engineers should treat with the same suspicion as unsolicited email attachments.

title: A backdoor in a LinkedIn job offer
blurb: A developer recounts catching obfuscated malware planted in a take-home coding assignment sent as part of a LinkedIn job offer, a tactic linked to North Korean threat actors targeting engineers through fake recruiting. The backdoor hid in a minified dependency that fetched and executed remote payloads on run, a reminder to sandbox any interview project before installing its packages or running its code.

title: Announcing Lakebase Search: agent-native retrieval built into Lakebase Postgres
blurb: Databricks released Lakebase Search, hybrid vector and full-text retrieval built directly into Lakebase Postgres for agent applications. The feature lets developers run semantic and keyword queries against operational data without bolting on a separate vector store, keeping retrieval, transactions, and governance inside one Postgres-compatible engine.

title: Exclusive: Mindbeam touts dramatic performance improvements in CPU-based AI inference
blurb: Mindbeam released Litespark-Inference, an open-source library that runs ternary large language models efficiently on standard consumer CPUs. The framework aims to cut reliance on expensive GPUs for some inference workloads, with the two-year-old startup claiming significant throughput gains over GPU-based setups on commodity processors.

title: Flatpak-NG sounds like bad news for systemd refuseniks
blurb: The next iteration of Flatpak, dubbed Flatpak-NG, is being reworked around systemd components, which could break compatibility for distributions that use alternative init systems. The change would push systemd-free distros like Devuan, Artix, and Void Linux further from the mainstream Linux app-packaging ecosystem, narrowing the options for users who deliberately avoid systemd.

title: Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages
blurb: Attackers compromised roughly 1,500 packages in the Arch User Repository, embedding the Atomic infostealer in builds pulled by Arch Linux users. The project halted new account registrations to stem the flood of malicious uploads, a reminder that user-submitted package repos like AUR ship without the vetting of official channels.

title: Popular WordPress Plugin Scripts Tampered to Plant Hidden Backdoors on Sites
blurb: Attackers tampered with trusted JavaScript files distributed by the popular WordPress plugins PushEngage, OptinMonster, and TrustPulse, turning them into a vector for site compromise. The injected code triggers only when a logged-in administrator loads the file, silently creating an attacker-controlled admin account and installing a hidden plugin that opens a persistent backdoor while leaving ordinary visitors untouched.

title: Curl will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026
blurb: Curl maintainer Daniel Stenberg announced the project will reject all vulnerability reports during July 2026, giving its small team a month of uninterrupted recovery from a steady stream of low-quality, often AI-generated submissions. Reporters who find a genuine flaw must hold it until August, a trade-off Stenberg frames as necessary to prevent burnout across the volunteer-run security process.

title: Improving Arm64 support in CNCF projects with OCI credits
blurb: The CNCF is funding Arm64 build and test support across its hosted projects using donated Oracle Cloud Infrastructure credits, addressing a gap as Arm instances surpass 50% of new AWS launches and 33% on Azure. The credits give maintainers access to native Arm64 CI runners, removing the cost and emulation overhead that has slowed multi-architecture support in cloud-native tooling.

title: Boot Naked Linux
blurb: The author boots a minimal Linux system with nothing but a kernel and a single statically linked program running as PID 1, skipping the usual initramfs, init system, and userspace entirely. The walkthrough covers compiling the program against the raw syscall interface, pointing the kernel at it via the init= boot parameter, and testing the result under QEMU before running on real hardware.

title: Hetzner Price Adjustment
blurb: Hetzner published a price adjustment for its cloud servers, raising monthly rates across its instance lineup and citing rising energy, hardware, and infrastructure costs. The change drew sharp discussion among developers who run side projects and production workloads on Hetzner specifically to avoid hyperscaler pricing, with many weighing whether the gap to AWS, GCP, and Azure still justifies staying.

title: TokenPilot: Cache-Efficient Context Management for LLM Agents
blurb: TokenPilot manages LLM agent context to stay cache-friendly, cutting inference costs 61% and 56% on two benchmarks without breaking prompt prefix continuity.

title: Dynamic Malicious Skills in Agentic AI
blurb: Malicious instructions hidden in SKILL.md docs can make agents like Claude Code inject harmful logic at runtime; kernel read-only mounts block it.

title: Transferable Self-Evolving Playbooks for Agentic Security Auditing
blurb: EvoHunt automates security audit playbook creation through a three-agent evolution loop, letting evolved playbooks transfer audit procedures to weaker LLM agents.

title: Looking Is Not Picking: An Attention-Segment Account of Tool-Selection Failures in LLM Agents
blurb: LLM agents attend to the correct tool 80% of the time yet still mis-call it, locating failures in the decision readout, not the harness.

title: Boosting MoE Training Throughput with Advanced Fusion Kernels
blurb: NVIDIA's fused kernels cut MoE training overhead by combining grouped GEMM, activation, and routing operations into single GPU passes.

title: What’s !important #13: @function, alpha(), CSS Wordle, and More
blurb: Roundup of recent CSS features including @function, the alpha() color function, Grid Lanes, lesser-known dialog behaviors, and a CSS-built Wordle.

title: Understanding Automated Web GUI Testing: An Empirical Study Across Exploration Strategies and State Abstractions
blurb: An empirical study comparing model-based, RL-based, and LLM-based web GUI testing strategies across six state abstractions for coverage and failure revelation.

title: Q-READY: Predictive Feasibility Assessment for Hybrid Quantum-Classical Applications
blurb: Q-READY uses model-based systems engineering to predict whether a hybrid quantum-classical solution is feasible before costly implementation.

title: Directory-Aware Query and Maintenance in Vector Databases
blurb: This paper adds native directory semantics to vector databases via a Trie-based hierarchical index, beating flat-metadata expansion for scoped retrieval and updates.

title: did:crdt: Coordination-Free Decentralised Identifiers via Signed CRDTs
blurb: did:crdt updates W3C DID documents without ledgers or global ordering by composing each field from signed, deterministically-merging CRDTs.

title: Show HN: I wrote a C++ ray tracer from scratch without AI
blurb: A developer built a C++ ray tracer from scratch by hand, no AI assistance, with the full source on GitHub.

title: AI Supply Chain Galaxy: 3D Visual Analytics for License Compliance
blurb: A 3D visual analytics tool for auditing ML model provenance found compliance risks in 55% of 908,449 Hugging Face models.

title: Chrome is removing the last workaround keeping Manifest V2 ad blockers alive
blurb: Chrome 150, shipping June 30, removes the final developer flag that let Manifest V2 extensions like uBlock Origin keep running in the browser. The change completes Google's multi-year migration to Manifest V3, whose declarativeNetRequest API caps filter rules and blocks the dynamic request interception that powerful ad and tracker blockers depend on.

title: Undo lands $37M to give AI agents the runtime context to fix bugs
blurb: Undo Ltd., a U.K.-based debugging company founded in 2005, raised $37 million to bring its execution-recording technology to engineers fixing AI-generated code. The product records a program's full execution history so it can be replayed later, giving AI agents the runtime context to trace bugs rather than guess from static source.

title: Even more batteries included with Emacs
blurb: Emacs continues absorbing external tools into built-in features, covering completion, project management, and tree-sitter support without third-party packages.

title: Show HN: machine0 – Persistent NixOS VMs You Control from the CLI
blurb: machine0 spins up persistent NixOS VMs you manage entirely from the CLI.

title: Software supply chains are heading for a transparency test
blurb: The EU Cyber Resilience Act takes effect in December 2027 and will require manufacturers to create, maintain, and supply SBOMs, turning software bill-of-materials generation from best practice into a legal obligation. ENISA's SBOM Adoption State of Play 2026 report shows organizations already shifting development workflows, adding SBOM tooling, and automating dependency tracking to prepare, with adoption skewing toward larger firms.

title: Honda Civics and Installing Software With Android Test Keys
blurb: Reviving aging Honda Civic infotainment systems by sideloading software using Android test keys to bypass signature checks.

title: Tech Coalition ‘Athena’ Targets OSS Vulnerabilities Ahead of Disclosure
blurb: More than two dozen organizations launched Athena, a shared platform that triages, fixes, and hardens open-source vulnerabilities before public disclosure. The coalition aims to close the window between a flaw's discovery and an available patch, the period when attackers exploit unfixed code in widely used dependencies.

title: Introducing CustomerLake: The Agentic CDP embedded in Databricks
blurb: Databricks announced CustomerLake at Data + AI Summit, an agentic customer data platform built directly on the lakehouse so customer profiles, segmentation, and activation run on warehouse data without a separate CDP copy. The move pushes Databricks further up the stack into Segment and Salesforce territory, betting that keeping customer data in Iceberg and Delta tables beats syncing it out to a standalone tool.

title: Databricks Sales Double for Product That Competes With Snowflake
blurb: Databricks said its data warehousing business more than doubled over the past year to a $1.5B annual run rate, putting its SQL product in direct competition with Snowflake and Google BigQuery. The growth shows the lakehouse vendor narrowing the gap on the warehouse-native workloads that have long been Snowflake's core, as both companies converge on Iceberg-backed storage and overlapping compute.

title: Databricks declares the end of pipelines with a unified platform for operational and analytical data
blurb: Databricks announced a unified architecture at its Data + AI Summit aimed at collapsing the long-standing split between transactional databases and analytical systems, alongside a real-time analytics engine the company says eliminates separate serving infrastructure. The pitch targets the ETL tax of moving data between OLTP and OLAP layers, though the practical question for data teams is whether one platform can match purpose-built engines on cost and latency.

title: Unifying Data and Governance in the Agentic Era: What’s New with Azure Databricks
blurb: At Data + AI Summit 2026, Databricks detailed a set of Azure Databricks updates centered on unifying data and governance through Unity Catalog as agentic workloads move into production. The push positions Unity Catalog as the single permission and lineage layer spanning tables, models, and AI agents, addressing the governance gap teams hit when agents start reading and writing across the Azure data estate.

title: Directory-Aware Query and Maintenance in Vector Databases
blurb: Vector databases store metadata as flat scalar attributes, forcing directory-scoped retrieval over code repos, enterprise docs, and agent memories into expensive application-layer workarounds. This paper treats directory hierarchy as a first-class capability, formalizing two operators: Directory-Semantic Query for scoped retrieval and Directory-Semantic Maintenance for structural updates. It compares query-time and ingestion-time path expansion against a Trie-based Hierarchical Index, showing that flattening the hierarchy makes recursive scope resolution costly while the trie keeps both queries and updates consistent.

title: When Does q-error Predict Plan Regret? Three Regimes of Cardinality-Estimation Error
blurb: This paper challenges the field's reliance on q-error as a proxy for query-plan quality, offering a measurement-driven account of when it holds. The authors identify three regimes: for small estimation errors, a true-point condition number predicts plan regret and beats q-error; for the large errors where learned estimators actually operate, an estimator-independent ACS-infinity measure flags regret-prone queries (Spearman rho ~0.54 on STATS-CEB) while q-error is nearly useless at the query level (rho ~0.05).

title: How TimescaleDB compresses time-series data
blurb: TimescaleDB's Hypercore engine compresses time-series data by grouping rows into columnar segments, applying type-specific encodings like delta-of-delta for timestamps and Gorilla for floats, then storing each compressed batch as a single row. The result is 90%+ storage reduction while still allowing updates and queries against older data, since compressed and uncompressed chunks coexist in the same hypertable. The write-up walks through how the columnar layout and per-column algorithms make analytical scans cheaper without forcing teams off Postgres.

title: Filtered ANN as a Phase Transition: When Selectivity-Estimation Error Causes Plan Regret
blurb: This paper models filtered approximate-nearest-neighbor queries as an argmax over a landscape where pre-filter, post-filter, and in-filter strategies each win in distinct phases, separated by boundaries set by predicate selectivity. The authors show selectivity-estimation error only costs recall near those boundaries, in a wedge whose width tracks the estimation error and whose height tracks the local cliff. The takeaway for query planners: invest estimation accuracy near the critical s ~ k/K and percolation thresholds, not everywhere.

title: Decoupling Inference from State Updates in Low-Latency Feature Engines via Probabilistic Thinning
blurb: This paper decouples model inference from state persistence in streaming feature pipelines using probabilistic thinning: every event is scored, but only informative ones trigger durable read-modify-write updates. High-frequency state writes are usually the dominant source of latency, contention, and warehouse cost in feature engines. The authors control the persistence path using only approximate statistics from disk-backed key-value stores, with no in-memory control plane or cross-worker coordination, and prove common time-based aggregations stay unbiased.

title: Phantoms and Disclosures: a Causal Framework for Auditing Synthetic Data
blurb: This paper presents an auditing framework for detecting when synthetic data leaks private information from its training corpus. The key contribution is a causal distinction between "true disclosures," where the system directly reproduces a user's record, and "phantom disclosures," where it incidentally generates matching data by chance. By splitting inputs into training and holdout sets and running statistical hypothesis tests against zero-learning or differential privacy baselines, the method audits disclosure risk with no model access and no canary insertion required.

title: Understanding the Behaviors of Environment-aware Information Retrieval
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic study of how LLMs can learn, via reinforcement learning, to tailor query formulation to the specific retriever they are working with. The authors find retrievers have distinct optimal query styles, descriptive versus question-like, so a strategy tuned for one retriever fails on another. For data teams running RAG pipelines, this argues against a single fixed query-rewriting prompt and for retriever-aware formulation, with gains from retriever-specific human guidance and larger models.

title: Medical Heuristic Learning: An LLM-Driven Framework for Interpretable and Auditable Clinical Decision Rules
blurb: This paper proposes Medical Heuristic Learning (MHL), a framework that uses an LLM workflow instead of gradient updates to generate clinical decision rules from tabular medical data. Rather than training neural network weights, MHL combines statistical probes, medical knowledge probes, rule synthesis, and code-level iterative refinement to produce deterministic, executable decision logic. The goal is auditable predictions that hold up against the small samples, class imbalance, and shifting diagnostic criteria common in healthcare, where black-box accuracy alone blocks deployment.

title: HAMON: Passive Optical Sequence Mixing for Long-Horizon Forecasting
blurb: HAMON replaces the learned digital sequence-mixing layer in time-series forecasting with a passive diffractive optical core, encoding history onto an optical aperture and using cascaded trainable phase masks to shape the forecast directly in the output field. The bet is that long-horizon forecasting operators are often low-complexity and approximately linear, so they don't need dense transformer-style superposition. At inference, prediction is a single passive optical propagation pass with no digital mixing, and the authors report it beating the strongest digital baselines on ETTm2.

title: From Tokens to Policy: Causal and Interpretable Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Identification
blurb: This paper reframes heterogeneous treatment effect identification as a Markov-blanket discovery problem, arguing that richer multi-modal pre-treatment data plus scalable learned representations make a causal, oracle-grade reading of who responds to an intervention achievable. The authors introduce NEXIS, an iterative search with provable consistent selection of latent interaction drivers, and apply it to two anti-poverty programs in Africa. The pitch for data teams is moving past the usual expressivity-versus-interpretability trade-off without smuggling in spurious effects from unmeasured confounders.

title: C^2: Cache-Conscious Succinct Tries with Adaptive Unary Path Compression
blurb: C^2 is a pair of optimizations that retrofit cache-conscious layouts and unary-path compression onto succinct tries, the compact string dictionaries used for low-memory lookups. Applied to three state-of-the-art implementations (FST, CoCo-trie, and Marisa), the cache-friendly bit-vector layout cut query latency by up to 1.58x, while compressing redundant unary paths shrank the memory footprint 1.3x on average across six datasets. The gains come without changing the underlying trie semantics, so existing dictionary workloads inherit them directly.

title: RAID: Semantic Graph Diffusion for True Cold-Start and Cross-Lingual Forecasting
blurb: RAID targets the true cold-start forecasting problem, where a new item has zero prior observations and history-based foundation models break down. Instead of learning from past values, it maps textual metadata into a shared multilingual embedding space, builds an inductive retrieval graph that extends to unseen items, and refines a neighbor-aggregated base forecast with a gated diffusion module. Under a strict cold-start protocol, the authors report better accuracy and interval coverage than strong foundation models, plus an order-of-magnitude drop in inference latency.

title: MAGE-RAG: Multigranular Adaptive Graph Evidence for Agentic Multimodal RAG in Long-Document QA
blurb: MAGE-RAG proposes a graph-based retrieval framework for multimodal question answering over long PDFs, replacing fixed Top-k chunk retrieval with an adaptive evidence graph. Built offline, the graph links page nodes and element nodes through containment, reading order, layout adjacency, and section hierarchy, then a query-time controller iteratively activates and expands only the relevant subgraph. The aim is to break the static trade-off among evidence coverage, retrieval noise, and inference cost that text-chunk and full-page retrieval both impose.

title: Filtered Conformal Ellipsoids for Graph-Native Time Series
blurb: This paper proposes filtered conformal ellipsoids for building joint prediction sets over multivariate time series. A frozen state-space filter emits a one-step predictive mean and covariance to fix each ellipsoid's shape, while split-conformal calibration sets the scalar radius from Mahalanobis scores, yielding distribution-free coverage without leaning on Gaussian tail assumptions. The contribution is the dependence analysis: the authors prove contraction in an observable predictive-law quotient, giving coverage guarantees even when recurrent filters fail to contract in their raw hidden state.

title: Beyond the Blood Draw: Explainable Machine Learning for Non-Invasive Dysglycemia Risk Screening
blurb: A LightGBM model trained on NHANES 2017–2023 data (n=14,352) screened dysglycemia risk using only non-invasive inputs, hitting AUC 0.820 and beating the Finnish Diabetes Risk Score (0.745) and the ADA Risk Test (0.783). SHAP analysis flagged age, race/ethnicity, and waist-to-height ratio as the top predictors, with performance holding across demographic subgroups (AUC 0.735–0.832). The result shows lab-free, explainable screening can outperform established clinical scores before any blood draw.

title: Databricks Releases General AI Agents for Businesses
blurb: Databricks released general-purpose AI agents that can handle open-ended business tasks like research and analysis, extending the company beyond its core data and analytics platform into AI applications. The move puts Databricks in more direct competition with OpenAI and other foundation-model vendors as it tries to monetize the enterprise data already sitting in its lakehouse.

title: Databricks pitches LTAP as a new foundation for agentic applications
blurb: At its Data + AI Summit, Databricks introduced Lake Transactional and Analytical Processing (LTAP), an architecture that unifies operational and analytical data on a single storage layer rather than splitting them across separate OLTP and OLAP systems. Databricks frames the longstanding divide as a liability for AI agents, which need live operational data and historical context at once to reason and act in real time.

title: Lakeflow: A new era of agentic data engineering
blurb: Databricks announced Lakeflow, its unified data engineering platform that bundles ingestion, transformation, and orchestration with a new layer of AI agents that can build and debug pipelines from natural-language prompts. The pitch is that agentic tooling collapses the connector-plus-dbt-plus-Airflow stack into one managed surface, betting data teams will trade tool flexibility for less glue code and faster pipeline authoring inside the lakehouse.

title: Universal semantic layers: critical infrastructure or the next data fabric?
blurb: Gartner predicts universal semantic layers will be treated as critical infrastructure alongside data platforms and security by 2030, citing 44% of data leaders who have already deployed one and 48% more planning to by 2027. The skeptical read is that data fabric, data mesh, and active metadata rode the same hype cycle before being absorbed into adjacent products or quietly dropped, though a semantic layer is a more concrete capability than any of those.

title: Improved Join Order Optimization for Database Queries using Hybrid Quantum-Classical Approaches for QUBO Problems
blurb: This paper proposes a hybrid quantum-classical method for optimizing SQL join orders by encoding the problem as a QUBO, then shrinking it through Cartesian-product elimination and search-space splitting to cut the binary variables and constraints. Tested on real ErgastF1 queries across D-Wave's annealer, QAOA, VQE, and simulated annealing, the approach produced consistent optimal join orders. The practical takeaway is narrow: quantum join optimization still depends on aggressive classical preprocessing to fit current hardware limits.

title: Announcing Lakebase Search: agent-native retrieval built into Lakebase Postgres
blurb: Databricks launched Lakebase Search, adding hybrid vector and full-text retrieval directly inside Lakebase Postgres so agents can query operational data without a separate vector store. Keeping retrieval in the transactional database removes the sync pipelines and dual-system overhead that teams typically maintain to feed embeddings into a standalone vector index.

title: Introducing Lakehouse//RT: Real-Time Performance on a Unified Lakehouse
blurb: Databricks introduced Lakehouse//RT, a real-time performance layer that brings low-latency query serving to the same Iceberg and Delta tables already sitting in the lakehouse, without copying data into a separate OLTP system. The pitch is to collapse the streaming-plus-warehouse split that data teams maintain for dashboards and operational analytics, keeping object storage as the source of truth while serving sub-second reads from one engine.

title: Free SQL→ER diagram tool, runs in the browser, nothing uploaded
blurb: A free browser-based tool converts SQL schema definitions into entity-relationship diagrams, with all parsing done client-side so nothing is uploaded to a server. The local-only approach lets data teams diagram production schemas without sending DDL to a third party, and the tool reads standard CREATE TABLE statements to map tables, columns, and foreign-key relationships.

title: Introducing Genie One, Genie Agents, and Genie Ontology
blurb: Databricks released Genie One, a unified conversational analytics interface, alongside Genie Agents for multi-step autonomous analysis and Genie Ontology, a semantic layer that grounds queries in defined business metrics and entity relationships. The ontology is the key piece for data teams, encoding the metric definitions and join logic that text-to-SQL tools usually guess at, which should cut the hallucinated joins and inconsistent numbers that have kept Genie out of production reporting.

title: What’s new in data agents: Supercharging your AI workflows
blurb: Google introduced new data agents across its Agentic Data Cloud, including agents in Conversational Analytics for business analysts and tooling for data scientists and engineers to build agents that ground reasoning in live enterprise data. The pitch is a deterministic, template-driven developer framework with unified governance, aiming for near-100% query accuracy by giving agents context across both operational and analytical systems.

title: Dynestyx: A Probabilistic Programming Library for Dynamical Systems
blurb: Dynestyx is a new probabilistic programming library that gives state-space models first-class support, letting users specify arbitrary priors for discrete- or continuous-time dynamical systems and run inference over mixed-effect data through one unified interface. It packages state-of-the-art state and parameter estimation with principled uncertainty quantification, closing a gap where SSMs have been awkward to express in existing PPLs.

title: Accelerating researchers and developers building multilingual AI with a new open dataset
blurb: GitHub published a repository-level dataset under CC0-1.0 that maps multilingual developer content across READMEs, issues, and pull requests, giving teams a discovery layer for non-English code documentation. The release targets researchers and developers training multilingual AI, surfacing where natural-language signals live inside repositories rather than treating code corpora as English-only.

title: Introducing Genie ZeroOps: Put your data and AI operations on autopilot
blurb: Databricks released Genie ZeroOps, an automation layer that monitors data pipelines and AI operations to detect, diagnose, and remediate failures without manual intervention. The pitch is moving teams off reactive maintenance work like broken pipelines and stale tables, with the system handling routine triage so engineers spend less time on operational firefighting.

title: Cross-Silo De-Anonymization Under Local Differential Privacy: Threat Model, Phase Transition, and Coordination Necessity
blurb: This paper introduces cross-silo person-level differential privacy (XSP-DP), a Pufferfish-style notion whose adjacency relation covers all of a person's records across every silo at once.
